title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the battery. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Stored Spark: The Evolution of the Battery
content.text: The story of the battery begins not with a laboratory, but with a frog. In the late 18th century, Luigi Galvani noticed that a frog’s leg would twitch when touched by two different metals. He believed this was due to 'animal electricity,' a vital force within living tissue. ||(41)|| [41]Alessandro Volta, a fellow physicist, disagreed[/41]. He realized that the frog’s leg was merely acting as a conductor and a sensor, and that the electricity was actually coming from the chemical reaction between the two metals and the moisture of the tissue. ||(42)|| [42]This 'Voltaic Pile' was a breakthrough, but it had limitations[/42]. It was bulky, leaked corrosive acid, and the current it produced quickly weakened. Over the next century, scientists like John Frederic Daniell worked to create more stable 'wet cells' that could power the newly invented telegraph lines, which required a constant and reliable flow of energy. ||(43)|| [43]The next major leap occurred in the 1890s with the invention of the 'dry cell.' By replacing the liquid acid with a moist paste, inventors made batteries portable and spill-proof[/43]. This led to the birth of the flashlight and the first handheld electronic devices, freeing electricity from the heavy glass jars of the Victorian laboratory. ||(44)|| [44]As the 20th century progressed, the demand for portable power exploded with the rise of the automobile[/44]. The lead-acid battery became a standard component, providing the massive burst of energy needed to start an internal combustion engine. However, these batteries were incredibly heavy and contained toxic materials that posed a significant environmental risk. ||(45)|| [45]The arrival of the digital age in the 1990s demanded something much lighter and more energy-dense[/45]. The solution was the Lithium-ion battery. By using lithium—the lightest of all metals—engineers created a power source that could be recharged thousands of times and fit inside a slim smartphone or a laptop. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the battery is the key to our sustainable future[/46]. As we move away from fossil fuels, the challenge is to create 'grid-scale' batteries that can store the energy from the sun and the wind. The simple stack of metal discs invented by Volta has evolved into a global industry that is literally powering the transition to a greener planet.

paragraphs.E: Indeed, [41] this biological theory [/41] was widely accepted for years, inspiring Mary Shelley to write 'Frankenstein,' a novel about a creature brought to life by a mysterious electrical spark.
paragraphs.B: To [42] prove his counter-theory [/42], Volta built a stack of alternating zinc and copper discs separated by cardboard soaked in saltwater. This device, the world's first true battery, produced a steady, continuous flow of electricity for the first time in history.
paragraphs.A: These [43] early industrial batteries [/43] were essential for the expansion of the British and American railway systems. Without them, the signals and communication lines that prevented train collisions would have been impossible to operate over long distances.
paragraphs.G: This [44] newfound portability [/44] also had a dark side. The mass production of disposable batteries created a mountain of electronic waste, leading to the first government regulations on the disposal and recycling of chemical components.
paragraphs.C: This [45] environmental concern [/45] led to a renewed interest in rechargeable technology. While Nickel-Cadmium batteries offered a partial solution, they suffered from a 'memory effect' that reduced their capacity over time, making them frustrating for consumers.
paragraphs.D: This [46] revolutionary chemistry [/46] has now scaled up to power the electric vehicle market. Modern car batteries are made of thousands of individual lithium cells working in unison, allowing a vehicle to travel hundreds of miles on a single charge.
paragraphs.F: Many [47] modern researchers [/47] are looking for alternatives to lithium, as the mining of the metal is both expensive and ecologically damaging. They are experimenting with 'solid-state' batteries that promise even faster charging times and a lower fire risk.

questions.explanation: E refers to the 'biological theory' of 'animal electricity' mentioned before the gap and its influence on literature.
B describes the 'Voltaic Pile' created by Volta to prove his theory, which connects to the mention of his disagreement with Galvani.
A expands on the 'industrial batteries' used for telegraphs and railways, following the mention of Daniell's wet cells.
G links 'newfound portability' from the dry cell to the consequences of disposable battery waste.
C explains how 'environmental concerns' regarding lead-acid batteries led to the development of rechargeable alternatives.
D refers to the 'revolutionary chemistry' of Lithium-ion and its application in the modern electric vehicle market.
